results.possibleIdentity: Bettina Brendel (1922–2009) was a German-born American abstract artist and lecturer active in the mid-to-late 20th century, particularly noted for her work in the 1960s and 1970s. Born in Lüneburg, Germany, she was the daughter of the Expressionist poet Robert Brendel and immigrated to the United States in 1951, later becoming a naturalized citizen. Her artistic practice was deeply rooted in the dialogue between art and science, specifically focusing on concepts from theoretical physics, quantum mechanics, and mathematics. In the late 1960s (1968–1969), she studied the history and theory of physics at the New School for Social Research in New York and famously met with Nobel physicist Werner Heisenberg in 1972. Her works, such as 'A Numbered Universe' (1966), often incorporated technological themes like binary code and pixelated patterns to visualize subatomic motion and energy. Brendel is frequently associated with early intersections of art and technology; she is featured in the publication 'Explorations in Art and Technology' (Springer) and her work has been highlighted in major exhibitions like the Getty's 'PST ART: Art & Science Collide' and 'Particles and Waves: Southern California Abstraction and Science, 1945-1990'. In addition to her studio practice, she taught modern art courses at UCLA Extension for several decades.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael Davis (born 1948) is a Los Angeles-based sculptor and public artist who became prominent in the early 1970s. He received his Master of Fine Arts from California State University, Fullerton, in 1971. In 1970, Davis co-founded '58F Plaza,' the first cooperative artist's gallery in Orange County, which served as a significant venue for the burgeoning Southern California art scene. His career is characterized by site-specific sculptures and large-scale public art commissions that frequently incorporate themes of science, astronomy, and architectural history. Davis's work has been featured in several major publications and is included in the historical survey 'L.A. Rising: SoCal Artists before 1980.' He has held academic positions at various institutions, including the Otis Art Institute and Claremont Graduate School. His connection to technological and experimental art is highlighted by his involvement in regional exhibits associated with 'Experiments in Art and Technology' (E.A.T.), particularly within the context of the Los Angeles art community of the 1970s. Notable works include the 'Ecliptic/Illume' installation at the Vermont/Sunset Metro station in Los Angeles and commissions for transit systems in Seattle, Dallas, and San Diego.

Michael Davis (1943–2012) was an artist and musician best known as the bassist for the influential 1960s rock band MC5. Active during the late 1960s and early 1970s, Davis was a central figure in the Detroit counterculture scene, which integrated music with political performance and visual arts. After the band's dissolution in 1972, he became a member of the influential Detroit-based art and music collective 'Destroy All Monsters' in the late 1970s. This group, which also included artists Mike Kelley and Jim Shaw, was noted for its experimental approach to performance, video, and zine culture, often blurring the boundaries between high art and rock music. results.possibleIdentity: Herbert (Herb) Aach (1923–1985) was a German-born American painter, writer, and color theorist active primarily in New York from the late 1960s through the early 1980s. He is best known for his pioneering use of fluorescent and phosphorescent pigments and his deep technical expertise in paint chemistry, which he developed while working for the Art Crayon Company (1954–1963). Aach's work, often described as 'color expressionism,' utilized highly saturated, intense colors to explore physiological color relationships and 'inner light.' He was closely associated with the intersection of art and technology, exhibiting at the Howard Wise Gallery—a hub for kinetic and technological art—and appearing in contexts alongside the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s. His professional contributions included serving as the president of the Artists Technical Research Institute in 1975 and publishing a notable translation and edition of Goethe's 'Theory of Colors' in 1971. He was a long-time faculty member at Queens College (starting in 1965), where he taught studio art and color theory, and also taught at the Pratt Institute and the Skowhegan School of Painting and Sculpture. Notable series of his work include 'Three Muses' (1967), 'Precession of the Equinoxes' (1974), and 'Split Infinity' (1980).

In the late 1960s, she began her 'Visual Philosophy' and 'Eco-Logic' concepts, notably performing 'Rice/Tree/Burial' (1968) in Sullivan County, NY, which is recognized as the first site-specific ecological performance piece. During the early 1970s, she was a fellow at AT&T Bell Laboratories, a hub for technological and artistic collaboration often associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. During this time, she engaged in computational experiments, such as 'Hamlet Fragmented-Wittgenstein’s “Pain”' (1971), and participated in the landmark exhibition 'Software' (1970) at the Jewish Museum, where she collaborated with computer scientists. Her most iconic work, 'Wheatfield — A Confrontation' (1982), involved planting and harvesting a two-acre wheat field in downtown Manhattan. Denes's historical identity is defined by her role as a 'visual philosopher' who uses analytical systems, such as her 'Map Projections' (started 1973) and 'Pyramid Series' (started 1970), to address global humanitarian and environmental challenges.

results.possibleIdentity: Ken Marsh (also known as Kenneth Marsh) was a visual artist, painter, and graphic designer active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He was a prominent figure in the early video art movement and was closely associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) network. Marsh co-founded the radical video collective 'People's Video Theater' (PVT) in New York City in 1970 along with Howard Gutstadt and Elliot Glass. The group was known for using portable video technology (Portapaks) for community activism and documentation. Marsh was also a member of the USCO art collective and was involved in E.A.T.'s landmark Pepsi Pavilion project at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. In 1974, he authored 'Independent Video: A Complete Guide to the Physics, Operation, and Application of the New Television for the Student, the Artist, and for Community TV', which served as a technical and aesthetic manual for artists using video. He also served as the director of Woodstock Community Video and lived in Westchester County, New York.

results.possibleIdentity: Gloria Holmes (also known as Gloria Graves Holmes) was a key staff member and collaborator associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during its peak years in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Based in New York, she served as the organization's executive secretary and administrative assistant, playing a vital role in coordinating the technical and artistic collaborations that defined the movement. She is credited for her contributions to major E.A.T. projects, most notably the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, where she provided technical and administrative support for the large-scale collaborative effort between artists and engineers. In addition to her administrative work, she was active in the community of women pilots (The Ninety-Nines) during the same period, reflecting the organization's focus on individuals with technical and scientific expertise. In her later career, she became a prominent educator and scholar, serving as a Professor Emerita at Quinnipiac University's School of Education. She currently remains active in the arts as a board member for cultural organizations such as the Hilton Head Symphony Orchestra and the Lean Ensemble Theater.
